questions.promptTex: \text{If } f \text{ is differentiable at } a, \text{ then } f \text{ is:}
hints: Differentiable implies continuous (the famous one-way implication).
Continuous at $a$.
steps: **Key theorem:** Differentiability ⇒ Continuity. (One-way arrow only — the converse is false.)

**Why one-way?** If $f'(a)$ exists, the difference quotient $\dfrac{f(a+h) - f(a)}{h}$ approaches a finite limit. For the QUOTIENT to be finite while the DENOMINATOR $\to 0$, the numerator MUST also $\to 0$ — meaning $f(a+h) \to f(a)$. That's exactly continuity.
**Why NOT vice versa.** A function can be continuous but have a sharp corner (like $|x|$ at 0), where the left and right slopes disagree. Continuous but not differentiable.
**Hierarchy.** Differentiable ⊂ Continuous. Differentiability is STRONGER — it implies continuity plus 'smoothness'.

choices: No — corner with different left/right slopes
hints: $f$ is continuous (no jump), but has a corner.
Left slope -1, right slope +1 — no single derivative.
questions.explanation: $|x|$ is continuous at 0 but has a corner. Left derivative -1, right derivative +1 — no single value → **not differentiable**.
steps: **Recognize the classic example.** $|x|$ at $x = 0$ is THE textbook example of 'continuous but not differentiable'. Worth memorizing.
**Check left and right derivatives separately.**
For $x > 0$: $|x| = x$, so slope is $+1$.
For $x < 0$: $|x| = -x$, so slope is $-1$.
**Compare.** Left derivative ($-1$) ≠ right derivative ($+1$). The slope doesn't have a single value at 0.
**Picture.** The graph makes a sharp 'V' at the origin — no smooth tangent line can fit, so no derivative exists.
**Diagnosis.** Continuous at 0 (the two pieces meet) but NOT differentiable at 0 (the slopes disagree). This is a CORNER.

hints: $|x|$ is continuous but not differentiable at 0.
Counterexample shows the converse is false.
questions.explanation: False. $|x|$ at 0 is continuous but not differentiable (corner).
steps: **To disprove a statement, find ONE counterexample.** That's how mathematicians refute claims.
**The statement to test.** 'If $f$ is continuous at $a$, then $f$ is differentiable at $a$.'
**The classic counterexample.** $f(x) = |x|$ is continuous at $x = 0$ (no jump, no hole) BUT not differentiable at 0 (corner — Q2).
**So the converse is FALSE.** Continuity does not guarantee differentiability.

**Stronger counterexamples exist.** Weierstrass's nowhere-differentiable function is continuous EVERYWHERE but differentiable NOWHERE — pure pathology. Continuity is a very weak property; differentiability much stricter.

questions.promptTex: \text{If } f \text{ has a jump discontinuity at } a, \text{ then } f:
choices: has a vertical tangent at a
hints: Differentiability requires continuity. Jump → not continuous → not differentiable.
Not differentiable.
questions.explanation: Jump = not continuous. Continuity is required for differentiability. So **not differentiable**.
steps: **Use the contrapositive of 'differentiable ⇒ continuous'.**
**Contrapositive.** 'NOT continuous ⇒ NOT differentiable.' (Same logical content as the original, restated.)
**Apply.** Jump discontinuity means NOT continuous. By the contrapositive, $f$ is NOT differentiable at $a$.
**Logical reminder.** If A ⇒ B, then (not B) ⇒ (not A). The contrapositive is always logically equivalent to the original. Useful tool — sometimes the contrapositive is easier to apply.

questions.promptTex: \text{The function } f(x) = x^{1/3} \text{ at } x = 0 \text{ has:}
hints: $x^{1/3}$ is continuous and smooth at 0, but its tangent is vertical.
Vertical tangent.
questions.explanation: $x^{1/3}$ passes smoothly through 0 but has a tangent line that's vertical (infinite slope) → not differentiable.
steps: **Investigate the derivative near $x = 0$.**
**Derivative.** $f(x) = x^{1/3} \Rightarrow f'(x) = \tfrac{1}{3} x^{-2/3} = \dfrac{1}{3 x^{2/3}}$.
**Behavior as $x \to 0$.** $x^{2/3} \to 0$ from above (always positive, since squared). So $f'(x) \to +\infty$.
**Diagnosis.** The slope blows up — the tangent line becomes VERTICAL. No finite-slope tangent exists → not differentiable. But the function itself is continuous (no jump).

**Distinguish from a 'corner'.** A corner has DIFFERENT finite left/right slopes (like $|x|$). A vertical tangent has SAME infinite slope on both sides. A cusp has different INFINITE slopes (e.g., $|x|^{2/3}$ at 0).

hints: Corner is where the inside of the absolute value is 0.
questions.explanation: $|x - 5|$ has a corner at $x = 5$ (where $x - 5 = 0$). Differentiable everywhere else.
steps: **General rule for $|g(x)|$.** Absolute value creates a CORNER at points where $g(x) = 0$ (the inside switches sign). Elsewhere, $|g|$ is smooth.
**Apply to $|x - 5|$.** $x - 5 = 0$ at $x = 5$. That's where the corner is.
**At all other $x$:** the function is just $\pm(x - 5)$ (a linear function), differentiable.
**Quick check by translation.** $|x - 5|$ is the graph of $|x|$ shifted right by 5 units. The corner that was at $x = 0$ is now at $x = 5$. Geometric intuition matches the algebra.

questions.promptTex: \text{Three reasons a function fails to be differentiable at a point:}
hints: Three classic failures.
Discontinuity, corner/cusp, vertical tangent.
questions.explanation: Three ways differentiability fails: discontinuity, corner/cusp, vertical tangent.
steps: **The 3 classic ways differentiability fails — memorize this trio.**
**1. Discontinuity.** Any kind (jump, hole, infinite) — no continuity → no differentiability.
**2. Corner or cusp.** Continuous but left slope ≠ right slope. Cleanest example: $|x|$ at 0 (a corner).
**3. Vertical tangent.** Continuous, smooth in shape, but slope blows up. Example: $x^{1/3}$ at 0.
**Reverse usage.** When asked 'is $f$ differentiable at $a$?', check these three failures. If NONE apply and $f$ is built from differentiable pieces, $f$ is differentiable at $a$.

questions.promptTex: \text{A function is differentiable at } a. \text{ Zooming in at } (a, f(a)) \text{ would show:}
hints: Differentiable functions look locally linear when you zoom in.
Tangent line emerges.
questions.explanation: A differentiable function is **locally linear** at $a$ — zoom in enough and it looks like a straight line, namely the tangent.
steps: **Intuitive definition.** Differentiability at $a$ MEANS 'looks like a straight line up close'. Mathematicians say: 'locally linear'.
**Zooming experiment.** Imagine repeatedly halving the window around $(a, f(a))$. A smooth curve will appear straighter and straighter — eventually indistinguishable from its tangent line.

**Failed cases — zoom in and they STILL look weird.** A corner ($|x|$ at 0) still looks like a V no matter how far you zoom. A vertical tangent ($x^{1/3}$ at 0) still looks vertical. A discontinuity still has a gap.
**This is the SECRET behind calculus.** All of calculus rests on the fact that smooth functions are locally linear. Linear approximation, Taylor series, Newton's method — all riff on this idea.

hints: Polynomials are differentiable everywhere.
Other choices have problems somewhere.
questions.explanation: Polynomials are differentiable everywhere. The others have a corner ($|x|$ at 0), discontinuity (1/x at 0), or domain issue + vertical tangent ($\sqrt{x}$ at 0).
steps: **Differentiability catalog — memorize:**
Polynomials → differentiable everywhere.
$\sin x, \cos x, e^x$ → differentiable everywhere.
$|x|, |x - c|$ → corner; not differentiable at $c$.
$1/x$ → not even defined at 0; vertical asymptote.
$\sqrt{x}, x^{1/3}$ → vertical tangent at 0.
$\tan x$ → vertical asymptotes at odd multiples of $\pi/2$.
**Apply.** (a) corner at 0. (b) undefined at 0. (c) polynomial — differentiable everywhere ✓. (d) vertical tangent at 0.
**Answer: (c).**

questions.promptTex: \text{If } f'(a) = 5, \text{ what is the slope of the tangent line at } a?
hints: Derivative at a point = slope of tangent.
$f'(a) = 5$ means slope is 5.
questions.explanation: $f'(a)$ IS the slope of the tangent at $x = a$. So slope = 5.
steps: **By DEFINITION, $f'(a)$ is the slope of the tangent line to $f$ at $x = a$.** This is the geometric meaning of the derivative.
**Apply.** $f'(a) = 5$ ⟹ slope of tangent at $a$ = 5.
**No additional info needed.** The derivative's VALUE IS the slope. Doesn't depend on the function's form, position, sign, etc.

questions.promptTex: \text{The function } f(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 & x \leq 1 \\ 2x - 1 & x > 1 \end{cases} \text{ is at } x = 1:
hints: Check continuity: both pieces give 1 at x = 1. Check derivatives: $2x$ gives 2 from left; $2$ constant from right — same!
Continuous AND differentiable at x = 1.
questions.explanation: $f(1) = 1$ from both pieces (continuous). Derivatives: $2x|_{x=1} = 2$ from left, derivative of $2x-1$ is $2$ from right — match! Differentiable.
steps: **Piecewise function — check continuity AND differentiability separately at the boundary.**
**Check 1: Continuity at $x = 1$.** Left piece value: $1^2 = 1$. Right piece value: $2(1) - 1 = 1$. Match ✓. Continuous.
**Check 2: Differentiability at $x = 1$.** Left derivative: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(x^2) = 2x \to 2$ at $x = 1$. Right derivative: $\dfrac{d}{dx}(2x - 1) = 2$. Match ✓. Differentiable.
**Both checks pass — continuous AND differentiable.** The pieces meet seamlessly with matching SLOPES, not just matching VALUES.
**Lesson.** A piecewise function can be perfectly smooth at a boundary if (a) values match (continuous) AND (b) derivatives match (differentiable). When only (a) holds, you get a CORNER.

questions.promptTex: \text{If } f \text{ is differentiable on } [a, b], \text{ then } f \text{ is also:}
hints: Differentiable on the interval ⇒ continuous on the interval.
Continuity comes free.
questions.explanation: Differentiability implies continuity at every point — so on a whole interval too.
steps: **Extend the pointwise result to intervals.** 'Differentiable at every point of $[a, b]$' ⇒ 'continuous at every point of $[a, b]$' ⇒ 'continuous on $[a, b]$'.

**Why options b, c, d are wrong.** Differentiability says nothing about WHICH WAY the function moves. $f$ could be increasing, decreasing, oscillating, polynomial or not — none of that follows from differentiability alone.
**Continuity is the ONLY thing that necessarily follows.**
